title: Production & Costs
description: Short-run production, cost curves, and the relationship between marginal product and marginal cost

questions.statement: In the short run, at least one input such as capital or machinery is fixed.
questions.payload: TRUE. The short run is defined by having at least one fixed input.

questions.statement: The long run is defined as a period of more than five years.
questions.payload: FALSE. The long run is defined by all inputs being variable, not by calendar time.

questions.statement: Total product reaches its peak when marginal product becomes zero.
questions.payload: TRUE. TP is maximised when MP = 0, just as TU is maximised when MU = 0.

questions.statement: Average product is calculated as total product divided by the number of workers.
questions.payload: TRUE. AP = TP / Labour, representing output per worker.

questions.statement: Marginal product is the extra output produced by adding one more worker.
questions.payload: TRUE. MP = ΔTP / ΔL, measuring the addition from the last worker.

questions.statement: When marginal product is rising, marginal cost is also rising.
questions.payload: FALSE. MP and MC are mirror images; when MP rises, MC falls.

questions.statement: When marginal product is at its maximum, marginal cost is at its minimum.
questions.payload: TRUE. The MP-MC mirror rule means their turning points are opposite.

questions.statement: A rational firm always operates in Stage III of production where marginal product is negative.
questions.payload: FALSE. Rational firms operate in Stage II where MP is positive but falling.

questions.statement: In Stage I of production, adding more workers is still highly profitable.
questions.payload: TRUE. Stage I has rising MP, so each new worker adds more than the previous one.

questions.statement: Total fixed cost changes as the level of output changes.
questions.payload: FALSE. TFC is constant regardless of output level in the short run.

questions.statement: The AFC curve is U-shaped due to the law of diminishing returns.
questions.payload: FALSE. AFC falls continuously and is never U-shaped; it is not affected by diminishing returns.

questions.statement: Average variable cost reaches its minimum at the output where MC crosses it.
questions.payload: TRUE. The MC curve intersects both AVC and ATC at their minimum points.

questions.statement: When MC is below ATC, ATC is falling.
questions.payload: TRUE. MC pulls ATC down when it is below ATC, and pulls it up when above.

questions.statement: When MC is above ATC, ATC is at its maximum.
questions.payload: FALSE. When MC > ATC, ATC is rising, not at its maximum.

questions.statement: Total cost equals total fixed cost plus total variable cost at every level of output.
questions.payload: TRUE. TC = TFC + TVC by definition at every output level.

questions.statement: The gap between ATC and AVC narrows as output increases because AFC falls.
questions.payload: TRUE. ATC - AVC = AFC, and AFC falls continuously as output rises.

questions.statement: Marginal cost can be calculated as the change in total cost divided by the change in output.
questions.payload: TRUE. MC = ΔTC / ΔQ, which is also equal to ΔTVC / ΔQ since TFC is fixed.

questions.statement: In the short run, a firm can change its factory size to increase output.
questions.payload: FALSE. Factory size is fixed in the short run; it can only be changed in the long run.

questions.statement: Average product is U-shaped, rising initially and then falling.
questions.payload: TRUE. AP rises when MP is above it and falls when MP is below it.

questions.statement: In Stage II of production, total product is falling.
questions.payload: FALSE. In Stage II, TP is still rising but at a decreasing rate because MP is positive but falling.
